intro: Python operates as a general-purpose programming tool equipped with highly efficient scientific data tracking extensions. R is built specifically as a statistical processing environment and vector-based analysis language for data scientists. This migration path is relevant when porting workflows directly to environments specialized for mathematical plotting, complex linear models, and academic statistics.

diffs.from: General-purpose programming with external mathematical tooling.
diffs.to: Statistical computation, analytics, and built-in vectorization.

diffs.from: Explicit iteration loops or external NumPy vectorized methods.
diffs.to: Implicit column-wide vector operations natively across vectors.
Vectors, Factors, Matrices, Lists, and Data Frames.

examples.title: Vector Transformations
examples.description: Applying numeric adjustments across an array of values natively.
examples.fromCode:
items = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
adjusted = [x + 10 for x in items]

examples.title: Data Frame Lookup
examples.description: Creating a tabular layout and querying rows based on conditions.
examples.fromCode:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1, 2], "val": [10, 20]})
subset = df[df["val"] > 12]
examples.toCode:
df <- data.frame(id = c(1, 2), val = c(10, 20))
subset <- df[df$val > 12, ]

tips.title: Shift to Base-1 Indexing
tips.body: Adjust all array traversal coordinates to start at 1 instead of 0 to avoid offset errors.

tips.title: Use Arrow Assignments
tips.body: Adopt the arrow operator (<-) for identifier allocation to follow standard R developer idioms.

tips.title: Avoid Iterative Loops
tips.body: Replace explicit loops with native vector operations or apply functions to optimize script processing speeds.

tips.title: Utilize Built-in Data Frames
tips.body: Use R's native data.frame and factor types without requiring third- party libraries like Pandas.
